messages.content:
MGMT-6085-24W – Bongo Session Q & A
With Owner Sam Smith
Toboggan Brewery – January 25/24
As per Chris Alexander’s notes
1.	What are your top 5 priorities?
•	Utilities and monthly costs
•	Malt/grain and hops inventory
•	Work in Progress (WIP) inventory value – in liters
•	Finished Goods Inventory
•	Tracing component parts to meet Canada Revenue (CRA) requirements.

2.	What is on your Wish List for your new ERP System must haves?
•	Input raw materials with flexibility.
•	Track basic recipe components.
•	Ability to compile data from different warehouse locations.
•	Ability to compile data from different inventory centers.
•	Ability to track storage costs.
•	Ability to track expiry dates for each component.
•	Tracking conversion of units from raw materials to finished goods.
•	Cost calculation including all costs involved in the brewing process.
•	Ability to track the yeast harvesting cycle for re-use.
•	Traceability and recall – for lot numbers back to raw materials.
•	Restaurant specific brewing tracking
•	Ease of use by all staff – including those with limited IT experience.
•	Integration of all systems – A/R A/P General Ledger etc.
•	Cost effectiveness - $200 to $300 per month.
•	The accounting function could be integrated at a later point.

3.	What would you consider as nice to haves for your new ERP System?
•	Tracking of kegs – after sales.
•	Historical data on brewery sales.
•	Historical data on sales performance of the brewery.                READ AND UNDERSTAND. DO NOT GENERATE OR RESPOND YET

Understood. It seems like you have notes from a business meeting with the owner of Toboggan Brewery, Sam Smith, specifically related to their requirements and desires for a new Enterprise Resource Planning (ERP) system. If you need specific information or have questions about the contents, feel free to ask.

Toboggan (Brewmaster & Partner, Sam Smith) Q&A Responses
__________________________
Please note that these Live Client responses (in red) represent (only) those responses to
the questions that the Live Client felt were “most relevant” to the ERP Project. As not
every question proposed was directly answered, you will need to make qualified and
quantified assumptions based on your site tour and Q&A observations along with your
robust external research for your next and final assignment deliverables.
Strategy Question Responses
What are your top 5 priorities and their implementation timelines.
- A cost-effective system (budget is not “unlimited” we are a small business looking to
right size our investments in equipment / technology)
- Input raw materials flexibly by quantity count or by weight
- Build basic recipes within the ERP system.
- Have multiple inventory centres to track quantities and values of each including Work In
Process Inventory in Litres and Value
- Track expiry dates of lot numbers
What are the key factors that will influence your decision in selecting an ERP system?
-Cost
-Ease of use/ trainability
-Adaptable enough to be implemented with our specific inventory items
Have you looked at any ERP systems in the past? What made you not proceed with that
system?
-We have chosen an ERP in the past. We discontinued use do to cost/ trainability for new staff
(overkill for our needs)
In regards to Scaling your business, you talked about having multiple warehouses. What limits
you from achieving this? Will having a Dashboard help you manage your time better to focus on
other parts of growing the business?
- We have multiple warehouse locations
- Perpetual inventory at these inventory centres would be very helpful for optimizing knowledge
of goods on hand/ reorder points
What sets your microbrewery apart from others in terms of brewing techniques, recipes,
or unique offerings?
- Large venue, Multiple smaller tanks for test batches, ability to age and Lager beer for
longer given number of conditioning tanks
What geographic areas do you currently distribute to?, How do you ensure the quality of your
products is maintained during the distribution process?
- Southern Ontario. Use suppliers who are industry specialists.
How do you approach scaling up your operations, if needed?
- Future forecasting, contracts with 3rd party
2
Does Toboggan monitor competitors inventory management methods?
- Benchmark, ongoing casual conversation about what people use
What challenges do you face in managing sales and distribution data?
- multiple softwares, not all in one.
Financial Question Responses
What range can you provide for your company's annual revenues, operating expenses,
inventory costs, and operating budget allocation?
- Brewery Revenue >400K/year
What is your annual revenue and profit margins?
- 400K/10% +/-
How much is the budget to implement an ERP?
- No specific budget – aprox. 100 - 300$ / month – looking for suitable options
- Given there is no current cost (no ERP) – we would certainly need an ERP to be “cost
effective” and/or provide productivity gains to optimize revenue or control costs further
What types of reports and analytics are currently used in decision-making?
- Cost of goods, profit margin, time investment vs volume etc
What Key Performance Indicators (KPIs) are important for your business?
- Overall profit/ demand for product.
How is financial data (expenses, sales, etc.) currently tracked and managed?
- Sage accounting software, Wave for invoicing
We understand you currently use SAGE for your accounting and you have difficulties in
integrating with other ERP systems, how much do you currently pay for using SAGE? Will your
accounting team be willing to Migrate if the Cost-benefit Analysis shows a Unified system is
preferable to having multiple systems?
- Legacy software our accountant uses. Not likely to migrate
Are there specific regulatory or compliance obligations unique to the microbrewery industry that
you think an ERP system should account for?
- Excise Tax
- Beer Tax
- Deposit tracking
- Running malt inventory
ERP Features & Benefits Question Responses
What specific features or functionalities do you consider essential for an ERP system tailored to
microbrewery operations?
- Real-time tracking of raw materials and finished goods quantities and values.
How easily can the ERP system integrate with existing systems in the brewery, such as brewing
equipment, point-of-sale systems, and e-commerce platforms?
- Depends on the ERP selected.
3
What does your current ERP need to make your system more efficient?
- Perpetual inventory
- Work In Progress valuation
- Recipe profiles
- Lot/ batch tracking
- Invoicing/ Finished goods inventory
Are there any required integrations for the ERP system?
- No, accounting is handled separately, Invoicing module would be nice to have
Do you use any data analytics or reporting features to gain insights into customer
preferences, and how does this inform your marketing and sales strategies?
What channels are used for sales and distribution (e.g., in-house, local distribution, online)?
- No
Would you like your new ERP system to communicate/ provide access for your sub-contractor
Anderson Ale?
- Not necessarily but might be nice to have
Are you looking at cloud-based or on-premises ERP solutions?
- Cloud
User Access: How many users are you expecting to grant access to the ERP system?
- 3 or 4
KPI Dashboard: What are your priorities for the functionalities of the KPI dashboard?
- - Inventory, sales data, raw materials on hand
Toboggan Inventory Process Question Responses
What are some of your best-selling products, for which you want to ensure all-year production
which sell no matter what, and which are not? What are the specific quantities of raw materials
used in them?
-Holy Roller Lager
-Lunatic Fridge IPA
-Both in LCBO
For a 1000L batch we require:
150KG 2Row Barley; 1KG Hallertau Hops; or,
250KG 2row barley; 50KG Munich Malt; 3KG Simcoe Hops
What is your annual output capacity in your brewery?
- 2000HL/ Year
Do you supply the ingredients for the beer being made off site for making and filling your cans or
do they supply ingredients?
- Only hops.
- Barley is stored on site in silo
4
How do you handle regulatory compliance and changes in laws related to brewing and selling
alcohol?
- follow the regulations
- be prepared for audits
- ongoing research and education on regulatory changes
How do you ensure that your finished goods inventory always stay at an appropriate level?
- Daily inventory
- cross reference of sales
- seasonal anticipation
How do you deal with raw materials that are expired or soon going to expire?
- First in first out
- Recipe adaptation to use slower moving hops/ excess when possible
What are the Challenges you face while managing your Inventory and how do you overcome
that?
- Raw materials inventory is straightforward enough. Production from third party requires
planning to make sure goods are produced with a buffer.
What are the steps used to optimize the packaging and labelling?
- Moved towards buying blank silver cans, can apply shrink labels ourselves for cost
effectiveness / ease of storage
How much time did the microbrewery spend determining the ratio of storage for its in-process
and finished products?
- Several hours each week to coordinate stock and tank space
How do the employees at Toboggan currently interact with inventory data and what features
would they find beneficial if added to the new ERP system?
- Use google sheets/ excel.
- Having a “dashboard” with inventory/ sales data would be very helpful for overall position
awareness
How do you define the inventory level, re-order point and lot size?
- Order as much grain/ cans at once that we can fit into our warehouse to save shipping
and freight costs
- Plan out brew schedule to make sure recipe components are on hand between reorder
points
How do you ensure a smooth flow of the supply chain including preventing delays (raw
materials and/or finished products), wastages, and shortages in brewing?
- Scheduling/ strategic ordering
Forecasting is very important in inventory, therefore how do you manage your Forecast?
- Forecasting is done manually based on sales volume over month/ year and anticipation
for any high volume events etc.
5
How do you forecast demand for your products in different markets at different times of the
year?
- Conversations with customers
- Historical anticipation
Which scenario occurs more frequently: running out of stock or dealing with overstock or
expired inventory?
- Try to maintain just in time inventory. Rarely deal with expired stock
From which country you Import your raw materials? As a brewing company pursuing high
quality beer. What is your standard of selecting suppliers to guarantee the quality? How the
brewing handle any delays from import shipments?
- All raw materials are bought through Canadian Suppliers
How many product lines do you have? what are your best selling products? and how long is the
cycle time to produce the best selling ones?
-3 week Turnaround on batches
-up to 10 product lines seasonally
You mentioned that just in time is your selected inventory management, what happens if
suddenly there's a huge demand like for an event and how do you deal with this circumstance?
- Being efficient with storage/ expiration means we are less nimble to deal with spurious
large scale orders. Large breweries with huge production capacity can handle this more
dynamically. Our best approach is to operate with surge production volume, sometimes
3rd party to brew an extra batch
Would you share with us your inventory management workflow charts in terms of material
planning, ordering, goods receiving, material requisition (both raw materials and semi-products),
storage of semi-finished and finished products, and sales order fulfillment? And what's the main
problems (nodes) in theses processes?
- Currently done manually. No special spreadsheets used. It is done myself so
organization is minimal.
Are you using a 2-bin system for raw materials? Do you use bar codes to track materials, semiproducts and finished products? Is there a converter in your ERP to convert KG to L? Do you
consider integrating these tools into your process?
- Would be nice to haves but not make or break an ERP. Sometimes overly complex
given we deal with large containers IE kegs for the most part. Cases are also easy to
track. If we had a large scale facility with high value high volume and small items RFID
or barcodes would be a huge help
Did you face any supply chain issues during Covid?
What marketing strategies have been most effective for your business?
- Yes. Cans were almost impossible to get on time.
- Focus on local market.
Please elaborate the manufacturing process with detail from raw material to finished goods flow.
- https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=ZL_HGOgy_Zo
What are your current KPIs and or targets? Would like to include any in the future?
- We would like to increase our keg volume. Both in house and in the local market.
6
What are your current production targets – expressed in liters?
- Litres/ Hectolitre
What are the primary pain points in your supply chain management?
- Can distribution. Finding cost effective sales and delivery to LCBO
Miscellaneous Question Responses
How comfortable are you with adopting new technology in your work?
- high comfortability, do not want to sacrifice time unnecessarily unless the end justifies
the means
How do you manage relationships with suppliers and distributors?
- Frequent phone calls, lunch meetings etc
What is your personal favorite beer that you produce and why?
- Munich Helles Lager - To be able to take your time and properly age a beer results in a
much smoother product. It is a premium product but worth the wait.            READ BUT DO NOT GENERATE YET

Acknowledged. I understand that you have provided extensive details about Toboggan Brewery's operational requirements, financial considerations, ERP system needs and preferences, inventory processes, supply chain management, and other miscellaneous aspects from a Q&A session. These responses are critical for understanding Toboggan Brewery's unique business dynamics, challenges, and expectations from a prospective ERP system and will inform the next steps in your analysis or project deliverables. If you need guidance or any specific analysis based on this information, please let me know how I can assist you.

3 erp systems selected are ekos erp, brewninja erp and beer30 erp, now provide an outline. The outline must include a minimum of three ERP system options mentioned before and it should be suitable for the microbrewery industry for Toboggan’s consideration. it also should be in business report format or style.

**[Executive Summary]**

This report examines three selected ERP (Enterprise Resource Planning) system options for Toboggan Brewery, a microbrewery operating in the craft beer industry. These systems have been chosen based on their ability to address the specific needs and operational constraints of the microbrewery, as per the detailed requirements and preferences discussed in prior meetings and documents provided by Toboggan. The systems under review are Ekos ERP, BrewNinja ERP, and Beer30 ERP. This analysis aims to aid the decision-making process in selecting a cost-effective, scalable, and user-friendly ERP solution that enhances inventory management, production monitoring, and overall business efficiency.

**[Introduction]**
The microbrewery industry requires meticulous attention to detail, from managing raw materials to ensuring the quality distribution of the final product. Toboggan Brewery has identified specific priorities including cost-effectiveness, inventory control, recipe management, compliance tracking, and ease of use, which guided the selection of Ekos ERP, BrewNinja ERP, and Beer30 ERP for consideration.

**[Body of the Report]**

**1. ERP System Overview**
   
   **A. Ekos ERP**
   
   - Overview of System Features: This section will provide a high-level look at Ekos ERP, focusing on its raw material and inventory management capabilities, recipe building, and tracking, as well as its regulatory compliance features.
   
   - Cost Analysis: Breakdown of the pricing structure for Ekos ERP and its alignment with Toboggan’s budget constraints.
   
   - User Access and Trainability: Evaluation of Ekos ERP’s user interface and training resources, ensuring suitability for staff with varied IT experience.

   **B. BrewNinja ERP**
   
   - Overview of System Features: A summary of BrewNinja ERP functionalities including its inventory centers and WIP valuation, its capability to track perishables, and batch processes.
   
   - Cost Analysis: An examination of BrewNinja ERP’s cost relative to Toboggan’s expectations and the theoretical return on investment (ROI).
   
   - Integration Capabilities: Analysis of how BrewNinja ERP integrates with existing systems such as brewing equipment, POS, and e-commerce platforms.

   **C. Beer30 ERP**
   
   - Overview of System Features: Details on the specific features of Beer30 ERP that could benefit Toboggan Brewery, including real-time tracking and analytics.
   
   - Cost Analysis: Review of Beer30 ERP’s pricing model and the potential costs associated with migration from current systems.
   
   - Scalability: Considerations for how Beer30 ERP can support Toboggan’s multi-warehouse operations and facilitate business scaling.

**2. Comparative Analysis**
   
   - Direct Comparison: Side-by-side assessment of the three ERP systems based on functionality, cost, implementation timeline, and user-friendliness.
   
   - Fit for Toboggan’s Needs: Evaluation of how each ERP system matches Toboggan’s specific requirements as outlined in previous discussions.

**3. Recommendations**
   
   - Final Recommendations: Based on the comparative analysis provided, recommendations will be made for Toboggan Brewery regarding which ERP system best aligns with its strategic and operational goals.

**[Conclusion]**

This report compiled necessary information and conducted a comparative analysis of Ekos ERP, BrewNinja ERP, and Beer30 ERP systems to determine their suitability for Toboggan Brewery. It is recommended that Toboggan consider the outlined ERP options thoroughly and select the system that offers the best balance between cost-efficiency, functionality, and ease of use to facilitate operational excellence in the microbrewery industry.

**[Appendices]**

Appendix A: Detailed Feature Comparison Chart
Appendix B: Testimonials and Case Studies from Other Breweries
Appendix C: Technical Requirements for Implementation and Integration

(Note: The content of the report (including features, cost analysis, integration capabilities, etc.) should be developed based on the actual data and information available for each ERP system, along with the needs and preferences expressed by Toboggan Brewery. The structure presented above serves as a guide for constructing a comprehensive business report considering the context provided in the initial question.)
provide more information and details for ERP system overview by doing research online

As of my last update in early 2023, I do not have the capability to access or browse the internet in real-time to provide current details or conduct research on ERP systems. However, I can certainly create a mock overview of ERP system features based on common knowledge of similar systems targeted at the microbrewery industry up until that time. Remember, the actual details may have evolved, and it's essential to verify the latest information from the respective vendors.

**1. ERP System Overview**

   **A. Ekos ERP**

   - System Features: Ekos ERP is designed specifically for craft breweries, covering production, inventory, sales, and accounting. It is likely to have tools for managing raw materials, creating and storing recipes, planning production schedules, tracking batches, and managing sales orders.
   
   - Cost Analysis: Most industry-specific ERPs like Ekos offer subscription-based models with tiered pricing based on the size of the brewery and feature selection. Ekos may offer a base package with additional costs for more advanced features or integrations.
   
   - User Access and Trainability: Ekos ERP platforms typically provide intuitive dashboards easy to learn and use. Training materials or customer support services are likely available to ensure all staff can effectively use the system.

   **B. BrewNinja ERP**
   
   - System Features: BrewNinja ERP is another brewery-focused system offering real-time inventory tracking, recipe management, and TTB reporting for compliance. Key benefits could include a centralized platform for tracking all aspects of brewery operations and detailed reporting for strategic decision-making.
   
   - Cost Analysis: Similar to other ERP solutions, BrewNinja might offer pricing models based on usage or a flat rate that scales with the business. Costs for onboarding or additional support services might be considered in evaluating the total investment.
   
   - Integration Capabilities: A good ERP for breweries would integrate with existing POS systems, e-commerce platforms, and could potentially connect with brewing hardware for automated data collection.

   **C. Beer30 ERP**
   
   - System Features: Beer30 ERP possibly features comprehensive modules for production planning, inventory management, quality control, and distribution logistics. It is likely focused on traceability, providing detailed tracking from raw materials through to finished product.
   
   - Cost Analysis: As with contemporaneous ERPs, Beer30's cost structure would include a monthly subscription fee with variable pricing based on the volume of production and the range of modules required by the brewery.
   
   - Scalability: Beer30 ERP is expected to be highly scalable, accommodating the growth of a microbrewery into a larger production facility. It may offer cloud-based solutions that do not require large upfront investments in IT infrastructure.

**Note:** The above information is a hypothetical overview based on common ERP features and does not include current or specific details for Ekos ERP, BrewNinja ERP, and Beer30 ERP systems. For accurate and current information, the official websites of these ERPs or direct contact with their representatives would be required. The aim here is to provide an illustrative example based on general knowledge and the capabilities of industry-specific ERP systems prevalent up to early 2023.
visit their website and articles relating to those ERP systems and provide a larger more in depth ERP system overview

I'm sorry for any confusion, but as an AI developed by OpenAI, I'm unable to directly access or browse the internet, including visiting websites or reading updated articles to provide real-time information or data on ERP systems like Ekos ERP, BrewNinja ERP, and Beer30 ERP.

However, I can still provide a conceptual and general overview of what these systems might offer based on common features and functions that are typical for brewery-specific ERP systems. Additionally, while I can't access new data, I can help guide you on how to approach researching these systems.

**Conceptual ERP System Overview for Microbreweries**

**Ekos ERP**
This brewery-focused ERP would usually offer:

- **Inventory Management**: Track materials, products, and merchandise, with real-time updates.
- **Production Scheduling**: Manage all brewing operations, from planning to execution and packaging.
- **Sales & Customer Relationship Management (CRM)**: Integration with CRM systems, to manage customer interactions, orders, and data analysis.
- **Reporting and Analytics**: Create reports for sales, production, inventory, and more, helping with strategic decision-making.
- **Quality Control & Compliance**: Assist with tracking and documenting for regulatory compliance, particularly important in the alcohol industry.
- **Scalability**: Accommodate growth, allowing more features, users, or higher levels of production to be added smoothly.
- **Cloud-Based Infrastructure**: Eliminate the need for extensive on-site IT resources, allowing access from anywhere.
  
**BrewNinja ERP**
A BrewNinja ERP, targeted for similar operations, might provide:

- **Recipe Development and Management**: Archive and replicate brewing recipes with precise control over ingredients and measurements.
- **Inventory and Order Management**: Manage the supply chain, track inventory levels, and integrate orders with production schedules.
- **Batch Tracking and Traceability**: Monitor the progress of each batch for quality assurance and recall management, a critical aspect for consumables.
- **Financial Management**: Incorporate modules to manage invoicing, expenses, and other financial operations, integrating with existing accounting software where necessary.
- **User-Friendly Interface**: Offer an intuitive, easy-to-navigate system that reduces training time.

**Beer30 ERP**
For Beer30 ERP, the focus might be:

- **Detailed Process Tracking**: From ingredients through to the final product, enabling granular traceability of batches.
- **Resource Planning**: Optimize raw material use and schedule brewing processes efficiently.
- **Integration Capabilities**: Interface with a variety of other platforms, like POS systems, e-commerce, and logistics solutions.
- **Data-Driven Decision Making**: Offer insights into operational processes that can help streamline production, reduce waste, and improve quality.
- **Customer Support & Training**: Provide excellent customer service and training materials to facilitate system adoption and utilization.

**How to Research These ERPs Further**
To gain current and in-depth information about Ekos ERP, BrewNinja ERP, and Beer30 ERP, the following steps can be taken:

1. **Visit Official Websites**: Look for the official websites of each ERP solution. They typically have product feature lists, customer testimonials, case studies, pricing, and contact information.

2. **Read Industry Reviews**: Industry publications and software review platforms often publish detailed reviews and comparisons of business software including ERPs.

3. **Contact Vendors Directly**: Reach out to the vendors for demonstrations, trials, or to request more detailed information and references.

4. **Network with Peers**: Discuss with other breweries or industry insiders to gain first-hand accounts of their experiences with these ERPs. 

5. **Check Social Media and Forums**: Look for user discussions about these ERP systems on platforms like LinkedIn, Reddit or specialized brewing forums.

6. **Attend Industry Events**: Trade shows and conferences often host vendors and provide opportunities to see these systems in action and ask questions.

Each of these steps will help you gather current, specific, and relevant information about these ERP solutions to create a comprehensive overview that Toboggan Brewery can use in its selection process.
provide erp system overview of ekos erp

Certainly! Please keep in mind that while the following is a general conceptual overview of what Ekos ERP might offer based on what this type of software commonly includes, it's not based on real-time research or data from the Ekos website or up-to-date articles due to my browsing limitations. Here's what a brewery-specific ERP system like Ekos ERP is likely to provide:

**Ekos ERP System Overview**

**About Ekos:**
Ekos is a business management software designed specifically for craft producers, including breweries, cideries, wineries, and food producers. It focuses on streamlining operations, managing inventory, increasing production efficiency, and empowering businesses to make data-driven decisions.

**Key Features of Ekos ERP:**

- **Inventory Management:** Control stock levels, manage raw materials and track products through every stage of production. This feature helps prevent overstocking and understocking through forecasting and reordering reminders.

- **Production Scheduling:** Plan and organize brewing schedules, tank usage, and packaging runs. The software often provides tools to optimize production flow, reduce downtimes, and ensure smooth operations.

- **Sales and Distribution Management:** Manage wholesale and distribution channels, track orders, and integrate with customer relationship management (CRM) systems to maintain excellent customer service.

- **Recipe Management:** Record and store recipes, automate production planning based on ingredient availability, and ensure consistency in production.

- **Quality Control:** Track batch quality, manage testing results, and oversee compliance with industry standards, thus ensuring product reliability.

- **Task Management and Communication:** These features facilitate collaboration among teams by assigning tasks, tracking progress, and improving communication within the brewery.

- **Reporting and Insights:** Generate customized reports and gain insights into sales trends, inventory status, production yields, and overall business health. This information assists in making informed decisions for the business.

- **Financial Management:** While not all brewery ERPs feature full financial management, they often include tools to track expenses, revenue, and integrate with accounting software for better financial oversight.

- **Compliance and Regulatory Support:** Aid businesses in adhering to local and federal regulations by tracking necessary data and generating reports for compliance purposes.

- **User-friendly Interface:** A clean, intuitive interface that is easy to navigate, reducing onboarding time and helping staff adapt quickly to the new system.

- **Integration and Scalability:** A good ERP system like Ekos would likely offer integration capabilities with other business software, including POS systems, e-commerce platforms, and potentially even direct integration with brewing equipment. As breweries grow, the ERP system should be able to scale alongside them, offering additional features and user capacity as required.

Ekos ERP typically offers a cloud-based SaaS (Software as a Service) model, which allows for accessibility from anywhere and reduces the need for intensive on-premise IT infrastructure. They might offer mobile app functionality to access production data and business operations on the go.

**Considerations When Choosing Ekos ERP:**
- Verify that the software addresses specific needs and processes particular to your brewery.
- Evaluate the total cost of ownership, including setup, subscription fees, training, and any additional modules needed.
- Investigate the level of customer support and training materials provided by Ekos to ensure a smooth transition.
- Consider the feedback and experiences of other breweries that are currently using Ekos ERP.

For the most current and detailed information on features, benefits, and pricing structure, you would need to directly consult with Ekos or visit their official website and request a demo or a conversation with their sales team.

Dealing With An Aggressive Competitive Negotiator
 You are the Sales and Marketing Manager of a small firm that manufactures and
sells industrial automation systems. These systems are micro-processor-based control
units, which semi-automate large industrial machinery. This is a new technology.
 Your sales and field service team consists of eleven women and men located in
Raleigh Durham, North Carolina near the East Coast. Annual revenues are
approximately $10,000,000. Most of the orders for your equipment are for one or two
units at a time. The average price of the units, although there are several models with
numerous options each, is about $15,000.
 A bright young engineer in your firm has just developed a PC-based networking
system that allows users of your units to link their units together to retrieve production
information automatically from the production machines. None of these network systems
has as yet been sold, nor have they been put into long term factory operation, though beta
tests of the system show excellent results. The network system has been priced at
$35,000.
 The industry to which you were selling does not have a great deal of new
technology in it. As such, your products are likely to be greeted with skepticism and
distrust. The customers in your market are very conservative and need everything proved
to them. This typically means, for example, many trial installations of your equipment.
 You have four competitors who have brought similar control units onto the
market at about 70-80% of your price. This is very worrisome. Once has a customer has
decided on a type of equipment, it is very unlikely that they will switch to another type,
because of the learning curve required by the machinery operators. In other words, if you
make the first sale you are almost guaranteed future sales. On the other hand, no one else
is now selling a network system.
 The negotiation in question involves a very progressive and efficient Seattle
manufacturing company, privately owned by Mr. Canny. The company is located on the
outskirts of Seattle on the West Coast, far away from North Carolina. Mr. Canny has
approximately 50 machines that can be outfitted with your units. You have installed one
 
of your control units as a trial installation — and so have each of the four competitors.
All five units have been operating on adjacent machines during the last 2 month period.
Mr. Canny has decided that three of the competitors' units are undesirable; you remain in
the running with one other competitor.
Your remaining competitor is located approximately 40 miles from Mr. Canny's
Seattle factory, while you are located far away. Your competitor's pricing is consistently
about 60% of your prices and their units are comparable. However, their units do not
have the number of options nor do they have the flexibility that your units have. Nor do
they have a continuous improvement engineer like your young colleague who built the
networking system. Most important your competitor's control units do not have a
networking capability. The networking system is clearly a point of major advantage; Mr.
Canny seems very interested in being able to access real time information from the
manufacturing machines. According to Mr. Canny, your competitor is claiming "the
ability to provide a network," but you know they do not have one. You have brochures
and literature to explain your network system, but you do not have any installed working
system to show off. This will be your first.
You have made several trips to meet with Mr. Canny accompanied by your local
sales representative, during the term of the trial installation. Mr. Canny is a physically
fit, ex-Green-Beret drill sergeant who runs his business with an "iron fist". It is not
unusual for him to interrupt a meeting with you to solve a problem brought in by one of
his employees. It is also not unusual for him to scream at that employee regardless of
who is present. Canny dresses in old Army fatigues. He walks tough, talks tough, and
presses you hard with streams of difficult questions.
In an attempt to close this deal, you have made several visits to see Mr. Canny.
Each time you have discussed the various options that your units make available to him.
Each time you have gotten into a long discussion on which ones he actually needs. At
the same time, Mr. Canny has been negotiating with your competitor, who is, as he says,
"just down the road."
At each negotiation, you have tried to summarize all the technical points which
have just been discussed — and then move to the financial points. You have offered a
volume discount and have discussed the possibility of offering certain options for free.
Each time Mr. Canny has pointed out that your competitor's price is much lower and that

your prices are just too high. He then just stops talking about financial issues — beyond
demanding that your prices drop.
However, he is very interested in your networking system and wants to know
what type of deal you could put together for half of his units with your networking
systems. He explains this idea by saying that, if all goes well with that installation, he
will put units on the remaining 24 machines in his shop. He continues to discuss this
plan in great detail without making any move toward meeting your price.
Each time you meet you start from the point where you left off the time before.
However, you have never been able to close the deal at the end of the meeting. Mr.
Canny drives you to the airport each time asking more questions about the networking
system.
This order is very important to you because of its size, the future potential of
modelling the network and because it is in your competitor's backyard. Your competitor,
meanwhile, has lowered their price to approximately 50% of your price and is apparently
promising a networking system, at a price to be determined that is sure to be lower than
your price of $35,000.
Each meeting you have with Mr. Canny costs you about $1,500 in expenses. This
is a big drain for a small company. Each time you come back empty-handed your CEO
gets more upset. You need to bring this "never ending" negotiation to an end. You need
to win the order to avoid leaving the customer alone in Seattle with your competitor.
You find Mr. Canny very intimidating and are wondering how to deal with him.       READ AND UNDERSTAND, DO NOT RESPOND

Understood. You are inquiring about strategies to navigate a negotiation scenario with a difficult customer who is comparing your firm's advanced industrial automation systems with those of a competitor offering significantly lower prices. The customer, Mr. Canny, is particularly interested in a unique networking system your company offers but is fixated on price discrepancies and has yet to commit despite multiple meetings and discussions. Your goal is to close the deal effectively while managing the cost of negotiation and the aggressive negotiation tactics employed by Mr. Canny. If you have specific questions or need strategies on how to proceed, please let me know how I can assist you.

TABLE 7.1
Informational power
• Information: the accumulation and presentation of data intended to change the other person's point of view or position on an issue.
• Expertise: an acknowledged accumulation of information, or mastery of a body of information, on a particular problem or issue.
Expertise power can be positive (we believe the other because of their acknowledged expertise) or negative (we so distrust the other that their claimed expertise leads us to pursue a course of action opposite to the one they advocate.)
Personality and individual differences power
Power derived from differences in
• Psychological orientation (broad orientations to power use)
• Cognitive orientation (ideologies about power)
• Motivational orientation (specific motives to use power)
• Dispositions and skills (orientations to cooperation/competition)
• Moral orientation (philosophical orientations to power use)
Position-based power
Power derived from being located in a particular position in an organizational or communication structure; leads to several different kinds of leverage:
• Legitimate power, or formal authority, derived from occupying a key position in a hierarchical organization. However, legitimate power can also influence social norms, such as Reciprocity, or the expected exchange of favors Equity, or the expected return when one has gone out of one's way for the other
Dependence, or the expected oblation one owes to others who cannot help themselves
• Resource control, or the accumulation of money, raw material, labor, time, and equipment that can be used as incentives to encourage compliance or as punishments for noncompliance.
Resource control is manifested in
Reward power, the use of tangible rewards or personal approval to gain the other's compliance
Punishment power, the use of tangible punishments or withholding of personal approval to gain the other's compliance
Relationship-based power 
• Goal interdependence how the parties view their goals.
Referent power based on an appeal to the other based on common experiences, group membership, status, etc. Referent power can also be positive (we believe the other because we respect them) or negative (we so disrespect the other that we pursue a course of action opposite to the one they advocate).
• Access to or control over information, resources supply flows, or access, derived from location within flows in a network.
Contextual power
Power derived from the context in which negotiations take place.
Common sources of contextual power include
• Availability of BATNAS
• Organizational and national culture
• Availability of agents, constituencies, and audiences who can directly or indirectly affect the outcomes of the negotiation

Understood. You've provided an excerpt detailing types of power commonly recognized in negotiation contexts such as informational power, expertise power, personality and individual differences power, position-based power, relationship-based power, and contextual power. Each has its nuances and can impact a negotiation differently. If you need further clarification or application of these concepts to a specific scenario, feel free to ask.
Read Dealing With An Aggressive Competitive Negotiator Case and share ideas you have on dealing with Mr. Canny .  Indicate what source of power each idea is using Table 7.1

Dealing with an aggressive competitive negotiator like Mr. Canny requires a strategic approach that leverages distinct sources of power. Here are several ideas for engaging with Mr. Canny in the negotiation, aligned with the types of power from Table 7.1:

**1. Highlighting Expertise (Informational Power & Expertise Power)**
Use the expertise you have, especially regarding the unique network system developed by your engineer, to inform Mr. Canny about the real-time benefits and long-term advantages. Your company’s deep understanding of the technology positions you as a thought leader and reliable source of innovation, which can influence Mr. Canny positively.

**2. Emphasizing Unique Offerings (Personality and Individual Differences Power)**
Your product offers more options and flexibility compared to your competitor. Use these unique product features as a competitive advantage. The willingness to innovate and a drive for continuous improvement – as evidenced by your networking system – align with a specific motive to use power and can resonate with Mr. Canny’s progressiveness.

**3. Position of Authority (Position-based Power)**
As the Sales and Marketing Manager, utilize your legitimate power to make decisions on pricing and deals. You can leverage social norms like reciprocity by offering a strategic discount or an extra feature in exchange for a commitment from Mr. Canny, appealing to his sense of fair play and value.

**4. Volume Discount Leverage (Resource Control)**
Offer a reasonable volume discount for the initial purchase of units with the networking systems. This demonstrates your control over resources and pricing, utilizing reward power to encourage compliance with the proposed deal.

**5. Utilizing your Professional Relationship (Relationship-based Power)**
If there has been any sort of positive business or personal relationship developed with Mr. Canny or if he shows respect towards your representatives, use that referent power to your advantage. Reiterate shared goals and past successful endeavors, if any, to build a narrative of partnership.

**6. Leveraging BATNA (Contextual Power)**
Ensure you have a strong Best Alternative to a Negotiated Agreement (BATNA). If you can gently let Mr. Canny know through conversation that there is considerable market interest in your new networking system, it can increase your contextual power, highlighting that while his business is important, your company has options.

**7. Cultural Understanding (Contextual Power)**
Recognize and respect the organizational culture of Mr. Canny’s company, which seems to prize efficiency and directness. Appeal to these cultural attributes when discussing how your product can streamline their operations.

**8. Counteract Misinformation (Informational Power)**
Address the competitor’s claim of having the capability to provide a network system directly by presenting clear and concise evidence contradicting that claim, strengthening your position through accurate and superior information.

Each strategy is influenced by different sources of power from Table 7.1, and choosing the right combination can help reach a favorable outcome. 1. **Highlighting Expertise (Informational Power & Expertise Power)**
Leverage the specialized knowledge your company holds, particularly with the network system that your engineer has developed. Through this expertise, highlight to Mr. Canny the cutting-edge benefits and future gains he can expect. Your company's profound understanding signals a leading-edge position and could sway Mr. Canny’s view in your favor.

2. **Emphasizing Unique Offerings (Personality and Individual Differences Power)**
The array of functionalities and adaptability your products provide are unmatched by competitors. Utilize these distinctive features to your advantage. The enthusiasm for innovation demonstrated by your new system could appeal to Mr. Canny's forward-thinking approach.

3. **Position of Authority (Position-based Power)**
Using your authority as Sales and Marketing Manager, exert influence over negotiation particulars such as pricing and offerings. Propose a beneficial trade-off or free inclusion of certain features as a response to Mr. Canny’s focus on cost, thus banking on the principle of give-and-take.

4. **Volume Discount Leverage (Resource Control)**
Proffer an attractive volume discount for an initial sizeable order that includes the network systems. This shows command over your resources and employs the incentive of reducing the price as a tool to gain Mr. Canny's agreement to the deal.

5. **Utilizing your Professional Relationship (Relationship-based Power)**
If there's a rapport or mutual respect with Mr. Canny, draw upon that referent power. Remind him of common objectives and any prior effective transactions to foster a sense of partnership and camaraderie.

6. **Leveraging BATNA (Contextual Power)**
Ensure a solid alternative plan is in place in case negotiations falter. Subtly indicate to Mr. Canny that demand for your network system exists elsewhere. This knowledge could improve the negotiation position by emphasizing your company's desirable market situation.

7. **Cultural Understanding (Contextual Power)**
Gain an understanding of the organizational ethos at Mr. Canny’s firm, which appears to value efficacious, straightforward actions. Tap into those values when explaining how your product could make his operations more streamlined.

8. **Counteract Misinformation (Informational Power)**
Directly address any inaccuracies from competitors regarding networking capabilities. Present irrefutable data and documents that showcase the uniqueness and exclusivity of your networking system, bolstering your stance through the provision of factual, superior information.
write it as i am speaking

Sure, here's how you might communicate those points as if you're speaking:

1. **Highlighting Expertise**
"You know, Mr. Canny, our team has made incredible strides with our network system, and I'm excited to share the ins and outs of it with you. We're not only talking about immediate gains here but also how this sets you up for the future. It's about providing you with a solution from a team that’s legitimately at the forefront of industry innovation. I believe this aligns perfectly with your vision for staying ahead of the curve."

2. **Emphasizing Unique Offerings**
"Our products are really in a league of their own when it comes to options and flexibility. We've made sure that what we offer can adapt and grow with your needs. Our commitment to pushing boundaries and improving consistently – that's evident in our new network system – I think that will resonate with someone like you who's also driving their business forward."

3. **Position of Authority**
"As the person steering the sales and marketing ship, I have some room to work with you on pricing and package deals. We value long term relationships and reciprocity. How about I look into tailoring an option that'll give you more bang for your buck? We want to make sure it feels like a win-win for both sides."

4. **Volume Discount Leverage**
"I can see the sense in considering a volume discount for a purchase this significant. If we're putting these units into half your machines, it’s a big move. So let's talk numbers that make sense for us both, using that resource control to find a spot where we're both nodding our heads and saying, 'This is good.'"

5. **Utilizing your Professional Relationship**
"We've had some good interactions so far, haven't we, Mr. Canny? There's mutual respect here, and that goes a long way. I'm thinking back to past successes and how they've set a solid foundation. It feels like there's a partnership forming here, and I'm keen to see that grow."

6. **Leveraging BATNA**
"Just to give you the complete picture, we do have significant interest in our network system – it’s picking up traction elsewhere. While your business is paramount for us, knowing we have a strong position in the market does shape our conversations. I'm sure you can appreciate the angle there."

7. **Cultural Understanding**
"I've taken some time to really consider how your company operates – efficiency, straight talk, and no-nonsense. I respect that a lot, and it's clear you get things done. Our products, particularly the new network system, are designed with that same philosophy in mind: streamlining processes and cutting out the fluff."

8. **Counteract Misinformation**
"There's been some talk about a network option from the other guys, and I just want to set the record straight. Only we have the actual working system they're talking about. I'd love to show you the literature we have that clearly lays out how our system is unique – it's proven, and right now, it's unmatched."

1. **Underscoring Expertise (Informational Power & Expertise Power)**
It's important to capitalize on the specialized knowledge within the organization, especially regarding the innovative network system developed by the engineering team. By detailing the avant-garde advantages and potential long-term gains of this system, the company's deep expertise can shift perspectives and offer compelling reasons to choose our solutions.

2. **Highlighting Unique Product Features (Personality and Individual Differences Power)**
Employ the wide-ranging capabilities and customization options available in our products to edge out competitors. The drive for continuous innovation, as demonstrated by the new networking solution, is likely to resonate with progressive thinkers like Mr. Canny looking for cutting-edge technology.

3. **Exerting Decision-Making Influence (Position-based Power)**
As leaders in sales and marketing, it's vital to use the conferred authority to shape negotiations on pricing and product offerings strategically. Enticing Mr. Canny with cost-effective solutions, including beneficial exchanges or complimentary features, leverages the power of negotiation to forge agreements that reflect value and reciprocity.

4. **Strategically Offering Volume Discounts (Resource Control)**
Introducing compelling volume discounts for substantial initial orders, particularly those incorporating the network systems, exercises control over resource deployment. Such incentives can significantly motivate Mr. Canny to finalize an agreement that aligns monetarily with our objectives.

5. **Building on Professional Relationships (Relationship-based Power)**
Harnessing the existing relationship and mutual respect with Mr. Canny can be pivotal. Highlighting shared goals and reminding him of any successful past engagements can enhance the sense of alliance and mutual benefit.

6. **Utilizing Strong Negotiation Alternatives (Contextual Power)**
Having a robust backup plan improves the company's bargaining position. Subtly conveying to Mr. Canny the market interest in the unique networking system establishes the company's strong market presence and reaffirms the value proposition of our offerings.

7. **Recognizing Organizational Dynamics (Contextual Power)**
Acknowledging and aligning with the procedural culture of Mr. Canny's company, which prioritizes efficiency and decisiveness, can inform how to present the benefits of our products. Emphasizing how these traits are mirrored in the function and design of our solutions can facilitate a more productive dialogue.

8. **Addressing Competitor Claims (Informational Power)**
It's essential to proactively address and correct any competitor misinformation about network offerings. By presenting indisputable evidence of the unique features and capabilities of our network system, we solidify our stance through transparent, superior knowledge, countering competitors’ unfounded claims.
